fields.name: 10. Build your Argument
fields.content-01:
0–5 min – Introduction

‍

  • Ask students: “What makes an argument convincing?”
  • Explain: In debate, an argument is not just an opinion – it must be built clearly and logically.

‍

5–15 min – Mini Lecture

‍
Present the four parts of an argument, with simple examples.

  1. Thesis (Claim):
    • The main point you're making.
    • Example: “Homework should be limited to 30 minutes per day.”

  2. Explanation:
    • Why is this point valid or important? Explain your thinking.
    • Example: “Too much homework leads to stress and less time for rest or hobbies.”

  3. Evidence (Proof):
    • Facts, examples, expert opinion, logical reasoning.
    • Example: “A 2023 study from the WHO shows that teens with over 1 hour of homework daily report higher stress levels.”

  4. Link to the Motion:
    • Connect your argument to the motion to show relevance.
    • Example: “This supports the motion because it shows that limiting homework improves student well-being.”

Show a full example on the board and underline each part.

‍

15–30 min – Group Exercise: Build an Argument

‍

  • Divide students into small groups (2–4).
  • Give each group a simple motion (e.g. “This House would ban homework.” or “This House would lower the voting age.”).
  • Task: Build one strong argument using the four-part structure. Use the worksheet for support.
  • Encourage creativity, but check for clarity and relevance.

30–40 min – Group Sharing

‍

  • Each group reads or presents their argument.
  • Give short feedback: Is the explanation clear? Is the evidence strong? Does it link to the motion?

40–45 min – Wrap-up

‍

  • Quick review: Ask students to name the four parts aloud.
  • Optional exit ticket:
    “Write one sentence for each part of an argument on the topic: ‘This House would ban fast food in schools.’”‍
fields.content-02:
Objectives

‍

By the end of this lesson, students will be able to:

  • Understand what an argument is and why it is the building block of debating.
  • Identify and create the four main components of a strong argument
fields.content-03:
Learning outcomes

‍

Students will:

  • Be able to construct clear and structured arguments
  • Understand the role of evidence and logic in persuasion
  • Practice linking arguments directly to the motion
  • Be able to identify weak or incomplete arguments and improve them
fields.content-04:
Materials and tools

‍

  • Whiteboard or projector
  • Sample argument(s) (printed or shown on screen)
  • Argument building worksheet
  • Prepared simple debate motions
fields.content-05:
Assessment

‍

  • Formative: Observe group work and listen to presentations.
  • Optional written task (exit ticket).
  • Peer feedback during presentations (e.g. “What was strongest in this argument?”)

‍

Tips and tricks

‍

  • Use real-life topics: It helps students generate examples and relate to the motion.
  • Model with color or structure: Use different colors or labels for each argument part.
  • Scaffold weaker students: Provide sentence starters for each part (e.g. “One reason is…” or “This matters because…”).
  • Repeat the structure often: Encourage students to always ask: “What’s my point? Why? Can I prove it? How does it support the motion?”
